canonical_definition: Across the provided papers, object hallucination is consistently defined as an observable failure mode where a model generates text referring to objects not present in the corresponding visual input. A less common framing expands this to include the neglect of visible objects. The field operationalizes this behavior using numerous evaluation instruments, with the POPE benchmark being the most frequently cited. POPE is described as a "widely used benchmark" that quantifies object hallucination through VQA-based tasks or binary classification to determine the presence or absence of specific objects. Another common measurement instrument is CHAIR, which is designed for open-ended captioning and calculates the proportion of mentioned objects that are not in the ground-truth image labels. Other benchmarks such as MME, AMBER, and MM-Hal-Bench are also used to assess aspects like object existence and count. This behavior is treated as a specific type of hallucination and is studied in the context of tasks like visual question answering and image description.
